title: PowerSpectR
text:
PowerSpectR is a lightweight R package for estimating radial power spectra from 2-D images with a median-based summary in Fourier space.
The current implementation is intentionally compact:

images are loaded with imager
tapering is handled with hann2d()
the centered 2-D power image is reduced with radial_stats_median()
global_psd() fits a simple log-log slope
plot_power_spectrum() renders the result

title: PowerSpectR
text:
PowerSpectR is a lightweight R package for estimating radial power spectra from 2-D images with a median-based summary in Fourier space.
The current implementation is intentionally compact:

images are loaded with imager
tapering is handled with hann2d()
the centered 2-D power image is reduced with radial_stats_median()
global_psd() fits a simple log-log slope
plot_power_spectrum() renders the result

title: PowerSpectR
text:
Method Summary
The package follows a straightforward analysis path:

Convert the image to grayscale.
Remove the DC component by subtracting the mean.
Apply a 2-D taper to soften edge discontinuities before the FFT.
Compute the squared Fourier amplitude.
FFT-shift the power image into a centered frame.
Collapse annuli with the median to get a robust radial profile.
Fit a power law after dropping the lowest-frequency bins if needed.

title: PowerSpectR
text:
Notes
PowerSpectR currently assumes:

2-D image inputs
radial bins that are linearly spaced in pixel radius
a simple linear fit in log space for the slope estimate

That makes it especially useful as a compact exploratory tool or a reproducible starting point before moving to a more elaborate spectral pipeline.

title: PowerSpectR
text:
COIN Toolbox
    PowerSpectR
    Robust, median-based Fourier power-spectrum analysis for 2-D images.
    
      Install
      Quick Start
      GitHub
    
  
  
    
    
      Built for astronomy, remote sensing, and texture analysis.

title: PowerSpectR
text:
What It Does
PowerSpectR turns a 2-D image into a robust 1-D radial power spectrum:

read an image and convert it to grayscale
apply an optional taper to reduce FFT edge artifacts
shift the Fourier power image to the centered frequency frame
summarize concentric annuli with the median, not the mean
fit a power-law slope on log-log axes
visualize the result with a publication-ready plot

title: PowerSpectR
text:
Why Medianized Spectra
The median radial profile is often more stable than a plain azimuthal mean when an image contains compact bright sources, masking scars, or a few localized structures that would otherwise dominate the spectrum.


Robust to outliers
Median annuli reduce the influence of bright point-like features and isolated artifacts in Fourier space.


Minimal workflow
The package exposes a short, readable function chain that stays close to the underlying spectral method.


Ready to plot
You can compute the spectrum and render the fitted slope in just a few lines of R.

title: PowerSpectR
text:
Quick Start
library(PowerSpectR)

img_path <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "example_texture.png",
  package = "PowerSpectR"
)

res <- global_psd(
  img_path,
  nbins = 72,
  drop_bins = 2,
  window = "hann"
)

plot_power_spectrum(res)

title: PowerSpectR
text:
Installation
Install from GitHub:
install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("RafaelSdeSouza/PowerSpectR")
library(PowerSpectR)

title: PowerSpectR
text:
Example Gallery
These example assets are generated from a synthetic texture included with the package website.

title: PowerSpectR
text:
Core Functions


read_gray_matrix()
Load a grayscale image into a numeric matrix for downstream analysis.


hann2d()
Create a 2-D "hann", "blackman", or "none" taper before the FFT.


fftshift_matrix()
Move the zero-frequency component to the center of the Fourier image.


radial_stats_median()
Aggregate the centered power image in radial bins using the median.


global_psd()
Run the full spectrum workflow and estimate the fitted slope.


plot_power_spectrum()
Plot the log-log profile and optionally overlay the image as an inset.

title: PowerSpectR
text:
Where It Fits
PowerSpectR is a good fit when you want a compact spectral summary of spatial structure in:

astronomical imaging
remote-sensing texture analysis
microscopy or materials imaging
simulated data products where scale-free structure matters

title: PowerSpectR
text:
References

Elson, R. A. W., Fall, S. M., and Freeman, K. C. (1989). The power spectrum of structure in irregular galaxies. The Astrophysical Journal, 336, 734-754.
Lazarian, A. and Pogosyan, D. (2000). Velocity modification of turbulence spectra observed in spectral lines. The Astrophysical Journal, 537(2), 720-748.
Starck, J.-L., Murtagh, F., and Fadili, J. (2015). Sparse Image and Signal Processing. Cambridge University Press.
